content:
# automation-failure-recovery-playbook - Overview

Automation type: verification_debugging
Tooling: Playwright, CDP, PowerShell, Windows
Goal: Recover from common browser and local automation failures with bounded retries and evidence.
Evidence: documented / high
Source: local-generated:2026-04-25/automation-failure-recovery

When to use:
- Selector not found
- Click intercepted
- Upload failed
- Login expired
- API down
- Path error
- Timeout

Inputs required:
- Error output
- Current screenshot
- Current URL
- Last successful step

AI summary: When automation fails, classify the failure, capture evidence, retry with a targeted fallback, stop on auth/dangerous ambiguity, and report recovered state or blocker.

# automation-failure-recovery-playbook - Execution Steps

Automation type: verification_debugging
Tooling: Playwright, CDP, PowerShell, Windows
Goal: Recover from common browser and local automation failures with bounded retries and evidence.
Evidence: documented / high
Source: local-generated:2026-04-25/automation-failure-recovery

- Classify failure as selector, timing, overlay, frame, auth, path, network, or permission.
- Capture screenshot/log at failure point.
- Retry with narrower locator or explicit state wait, not blind repetition.
- Check frame/popup/overlay context.
- For upload failures, verify path and actual file input.
- For auth failures, stop and report login requirement.
- For irreversible action ambiguity, stop and ask for confirmation.

AI summary: When automation fails, classify the failure, capture evidence, retry with a targeted fallback, stop on auth/dangerous ambiguity, and report recovered state or blocker.

AI summary: When automation fails, classify the failure, capture evidence, retry with a targeted fallback, stop on auth/dangerous ambiguity, and report recovered state or blocker.

# automation-failure-recovery-playbook - Safety Notes

- Stop on auth/payment/delete/publish ambiguity.

AI summary: When automation fails, classify the failure, capture evidence, retry with a targeted fallback, stop on auth/dangerous ambiguity, and report recovered state or blocker.

# automation-handoff-evidence-report - Overview

Automation type: verification_debugging
Tooling: Playwright, CDP, PowerShell, local agents
Goal: Require automation agents to report objective evidence rather than vague completion claims.
Evidence: documented / high
Source: local-generated:2026-04-25/automation-evidence-report

When to use:
- Any browser/local automation task
- Task changes remote website/admin state
- User needs proof of completion

Inputs required:
- Task scope
- Target URL/app
- Files used
- Expected success criteria

AI summary: Automation handoff must include target, inputs, steps, objective evidence, retries, skipped dangerous actions, and remaining gaps. Do not claim success without proof.

# automation-handoff-evidence-report - Execution Steps

Automation type: verification_debugging
Tooling: Playwright, CDP, PowerShell, local agents
Goal: Require automation agents to report objective evidence rather than vague completion claims.
Evidence: documented / high
Source: local-generated:2026-04-25/automation-evidence-report

- Report target URL/app and environment.
- List key steps performed.
- Include screenshots/logs/assertions for success.
- List skipped dangerous actions and confirmations needed.
- Report errors, retries, and remaining gaps.

AI summary: Automation handoff must include target, inputs, steps, objective evidence, retries, skipped dangerous actions, and remaining gaps. Do not claim success without proof.

# chrome-cdp-existing-browser-session - Overview

Automation type: browser_cdp
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP, Playwright
Goal: Connect automation to an existing logged-in Chromium browser through a local remote debugging port.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

When to use:
- Need existing login/session
- MFA makes fresh browser login hard
- Need inspectable live browser
- Admin console automation

Inputs required:
- Browser launched with remote debugging
- Local port
- User data directory/profile
- Target URL

AI summary: Use CDP when an agent must control an already logged-in Chrome/Edge session: launch with localhost remote-debugging port, verify /json/version, connect, confirm account, and close the port after use.

# chrome-cdp-existing-browser-session - Execution Steps

Automation type: browser_cdp
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP, Playwright
Goal: Connect automation to an existing logged-in Chromium browser through a local remote debugging port.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

- Start Chrome/Edge with remote debugging bound to localhost and a dedicated user-data-dir/profile.
- Check http://127.0.0.1:{port}/json/version or /json/list.
- Connect Playwright or CDP client to the browser WebSocket endpoint.
- Reuse logged-in state but verify account/profile before acting.
- Close debug browser or port when done.

AI summary: Use CDP when an agent must control an already logged-in Chrome/Edge session: launch with localhost remote-debugging port, verify /json/version, connect, confirm account, and close the port after use.

# chrome-cdp-existing-browser-session - Commands or APIs

Automation type: browser_cdp
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP, Playwright
Goal: Connect automation to an existing logged-in Chromium browser through a local remote debugging port.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

- --remote-debugging-port=9222
- --remote-debugging-address=127.0.0.1
- http://127.0.0.1:9222/json/version
- chromium.connectOverCDP(...)

AI summary: Use CDP when an agent must control an already logged-in Chrome/Edge session: launch with localhost remote-debugging port, verify /json/version, connect, confirm account, and close the port after use.

# chrome-cdp-existing-browser-session - Safety Notes

Automation type: browser_cdp
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP, Playwright
Goal: Connect automation to an existing logged-in Chromium browser through a local remote debugging port.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

- Bind debugging port to 127.0.0.1 only.
- Do not expose CDP port to network.
- Confirm account before irreversible actions.
- Close debug session after task.

AI summary: Use CDP when an agent must control an already logged-in Chrome/Edge session: launch with localhost remote-debugging port, verify /json/version, connect, confirm account, and close the port after use.

# chrome-remote-debugging-security-boundary - Overview

Automation type: safety_protocol
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP
Goal: Prevent remote debugging from becoming a local security risk.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

When to use:
- Launching Chrome with remote debugging
- Agent controls browser
- Sensitive logged-in websites

Inputs required:
- Port
- Binding address
- Browser profile
- Task scope

AI summary: CDP is powerful browser control. Bind remote debugging to 127.0.0.1, use dedicated profiles where possible, never expose the WebSocket endpoint, and stop before irreversible actions.

# chrome-remote-debugging-security-boundary - Execution Steps

Automation type: safety_protocol
Tooling: Chrome, Edge, CDP
Goal: Prevent remote debugging from becoming a local security risk.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/remote-debugging

- Bind to localhost only.
- Use a dedicated automation profile when possible.
- Avoid leaving remote debugging browser open after task.
- Do not share WebSocket endpoint or expose port through firewall/tunnel.
- Pause before destructive actions, purchases, deletes, or publishing.

AI summary: CDP is powerful browser control. Bind remote debugging to 127.0.0.1, use dedicated profiles where possible, never expose the WebSocket endpoint, and stop before irreversible actions.

- netstat / Get-NetTCPConnection to verify listener
- Stop browser/process after task

AI summary: CDP is powerful browser control. Bind remote debugging to 127.0.0.1, use dedicated profiles where possible, never expose the WebSocket endpoint, and stop before irreversible actions.

# playwright-auto-wait-actionability - Overview

Automation type: browser_playwright
Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Rely on Playwright auto-waiting and actionability checks instead of manual sleep-heavy automation.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/actionability

When to use:
- Clicks fail intermittently
- Page needs waiting
- Form submit or navigation is flaky

Inputs required:
- Target action
- Expected post-action state
- Timeout budget

AI summary: Use Playwright auto-waiting/actionability and auto-retrying assertions. Avoid fixed sleeps; wait for specific UI state, URL, response, or locator.

# playwright-auto-wait-actionability - Execution Steps

Automation type: browser_playwright
Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Rely on Playwright auto-waiting and actionability checks instead of manual sleep-heavy automation.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/actionability

- Use locators directly; Playwright waits for visible/enabled/stable where relevant.
- Prefer auto-retrying expect assertions for final state.
- Wait for specific UI state, URL, response, or locator rather than fixed time.
- Use explicit waits only for known asynchronous boundaries.

AI summary: Use Playwright auto-waiting/actionability and auto-retrying assertions. Avoid fixed sleeps; wait for specific UI state, URL, response, or locator.

Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/actionability

- await locator.click()
- await expect(locator).toBeVisible()
- await expect(page).toHaveURL(...)

AI summary: Use Playwright auto-waiting/actionability and auto-retrying assertions. Avoid fixed sleeps; wait for specific UI state, URL, response, or locator.

# playwright-file-upload-patterns - Overview

Automation type: file_upload
Tooling: Playwright, Windows paths
Goal: Upload files reliably through file inputs, file chooser events, iframe-aware locators, and upload verification.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/input

When to use:
- Need to upload image/video/pdf
- Upload button opens OS file picker
- Styled dropzone hides input
- Upload lives inside iframe

Inputs required:
- Absolute file path
- Expected accepted file types
- Upload success indicator
- Frame/dropzone locator

AI summary: For file upload, verify the path, target the real input[type=file] or filechooser, handle iframe context, then wait for filename/progress/success evidence.

# playwright-file-upload-patterns - Execution Steps

Automation type: file_upload
Tooling: Playwright, Windows paths
Goal: Upload files reliably through file inputs, file chooser events, iframe-aware locators, and upload verification.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/input

- Verify file exists before upload.
- Prefer locator.setInputFiles on the actual input[type=file].
- If a file chooser opens, wait for page.waitForEvent('filechooser') then setFiles.
- For iframes, locate frame first then target the input inside it.
- After setting file, wait for filename/progress/success state.

AI summary: For file upload, verify the path, target the real input[type=file] or filechooser, handle iframe context, then wait for filename/progress/success evidence.

# playwright-file-upload-patterns - Commands or APIs

Automation type: file_upload
Tooling: Playwright, Windows paths
Goal: Upload files reliably through file inputs, file chooser events, iframe-aware locators, and upload verification.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/input

- locator.setInputFiles(path)
- page.waitForEvent('filechooser')
- fileChooser.setFiles(path)
- frameLocator(...).locator('input[type=file]')

AI summary: For file upload, verify the path, target the real input[type=file] or filechooser, handle iframe context, then wait for filename/progress/success evidence.

# playwright-locator-best-practices - Overview

Automation type: browser_playwright
Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Use resilient locators and avoid brittle selectors in browser automation.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/best-practices

When to use:
- Automating web UI
- Selectors are flaky
- Agent needs to click/fill stable elements

Inputs required:
- Target URL
- User-visible labels/roles
- Stable test ids if available

AI summary: Use Playwright resilient locators: role, label, text, placeholder, test id, and scoped locators. Avoid brittle CSS/XPath and arbitrary sleeps.

# playwright-locator-best-practices - Execution Steps

Automation type: browser_playwright
Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Use resilient locators and avoid brittle selectors in browser automation.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/best-practices

- Prefer role, text, label, placeholder, and test id locators over CSS/XPath.
- Use Playwright Inspector/codegen only as a starting point; review locator semantics.
- Scope locators to a region when text repeats.
- Avoid selecting by dynamic class names or layout-dependent nth-child selectors.

AI summary: Use Playwright resilient locators: role, label, text, placeholder, test id, and scoped locators. Avoid brittle CSS/XPath and arbitrary sleeps.

# playwright-locator-best-practices - Commands or APIs

Automation type: browser_playwright
Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Use resilient locators and avoid brittle selectors in browser automation.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/best-practices

- page.getByRole(...)
- page.getByLabel(...)
- page.getByText(...)
- locator.filter(...)

AI summary: Use Playwright resilient locators: role, label, text, placeholder, test id, and scoped locators. Avoid brittle CSS/XPath and arbitrary sleeps.

Tooling: Playwright
Goal: Handle overlays, modals, dropdowns, popups, and iframes without brittle clicks.

# windows-powershell-quoting-and-paths - Overview

Automation type: windows_shell
Tooling: PowerShell, Windows, Python
Goal: Run Windows local commands with safe quoting, literal paths, and shell-aware path handling.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_quoting_rules

When to use:
- Windows path contains backslashes/spaces/Chinese
- Bash ate backslashes
- PowerShell command has quotes
- Need to run Python scripts

Inputs required:
- Shell type
- Target path
- Command arguments

AI summary: On Windows, use PowerShell -NoProfile and -LiteralPath for exact paths; in Bash use forward slashes. Avoid raw backslash Windows paths in Bash and report corrected commands.

# windows-powershell-quoting-and-paths - Execution Steps

Automation type: windows_shell
Tooling: PowerShell, Windows, Python
Goal: Run Windows local commands with safe quoting, literal paths, and shell-aware path handling.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_quoting_rules

- Use PowerShell -NoProfile to avoid startup hook noise when needed.
- Use -LiteralPath for exact filesystem paths.
- Use single quotes for literal PowerShell strings; double quotes when variable expansion is needed.
- In Bash/Git Bash use forward slashes such as E:/DataBase/..., not raw E:\DataBase\...
- Quote paths with spaces and avoid composing destructive commands across shells.

AI summary: On Windows, use PowerShell -NoProfile and -LiteralPath for exact paths; in Bash use forward slashes. Avoid raw backslash Windows paths in Bash and report corrected commands.

# windows-powershell-quoting-and-paths - Commands or APIs

Automation type: windows_shell
Tooling: PowerShell, Windows, Python
Goal: Run Windows local commands with safe quoting, literal paths, and shell-aware path handling.
Evidence: official / high
Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_quoting_rules

- Get-Content -LiteralPath 'E:\DataBase\...'
- python 'E:\DataBase\script.py'
- E:/DataBase/script.py in Bash
- pwsh -NoProfile -Command ...

AI summary: On Windows, use PowerShell -NoProfile and -LiteralPath for exact paths; in Bash use forward slashes. Avoid raw backslash Windows paths in Bash and report corrected commands.

# windows-process-port-service-control - Execution Steps

Automation type: windows_shell
Tooling: PowerShell, uvicorn, FastAPI
Goal: Start, verify, and stop local services such as the knowledge API without losing control of the shell.
Evidence: tested / high
Source: local-generated:2026-04-25/windows-service-control

- Start service in a dedicated terminal or background job.
- Wait briefly and call health endpoint.
- If port is busy, identify listener before killing anything.
- Use a different port for temporary tests.
- Stop test jobs after verification.

AI summary: For local services, start in a controlled terminal/job, verify /health, inspect port conflicts before stopping processes, and report health response and shutdown state.
